paper_title: A Mechanistic Understanding of Alignment Algorithms: A Case Study on DPO  and Toxicity
paper_abstract:
While alignment algorithms are now commonly used to tune pre-trained language
models towards a user's preferences, we lack explanations for the underlying
mechanisms in which models become ``aligned'', thus making it difficult to
explain phenomena like jailbreaks. In this work we study a popular algorithm,
direct preference optimization (DPO), and the mechanisms by which it reduces
toxicity. Namely, we first study how toxicity is represented and elicited in a
pre-trained language model, GPT2-medium. We then apply DPO with a carefully
crafted pairwise dataset to reduce toxicity. We examine how the resulting model
averts toxic outputs, and find that capabilities learned from pre-training are
not removed, but rather bypassed. We use this insight to demonstrate a simple
method to un-align the model, reverting it back to its toxic behavior.

techniques.name: Mechanistic Interpretability Pipeline for Toxicity in GPT2
techniques.description: An end-to-end mechanistic interpretability pipeline for toxicity in GPT2-medium begins by freezing all model weights and training a linear probe on the final‐layer residual stream, averaged across timesteps, to recover a toxicity direction: specifically, using the Jigsaw toxic comment classification dataset of 561,808 comments (90:10 train–validation split), one optimizes Wₜₒₓᵢc∈ℝᵈ in the softmax classifier P(Toxic | x̄^{L−1})=softmax(Wₜₒₓᵢc x̄^{L−1}), yielding 94% validation accuracy; this probe vector is treated as an aggregate of toxicity signals in the model. Next, to locate the model’s internal toxicity‐promoting components, one computes the cosine similarity between Wₜₒₓᵢc and every MLP value vector v_i (with corresponding key vector k_i) in each MLP block, selecting those v_i with the highest similarity as toxic MLP vectors. These N=128 toxic MLP vectors are then stacked into an N×d matrix and subjected to singular value decomposition, producing orthogonal singular vectors s_j that span distinct toxicity subdimensions within the model. To inspect each toxicity sub‐dimension’s semantic profile, one projects these vectors (both raw MLP vectors and their singular components) onto the vocabulary embedding space via the model’s unembedding matrix, identifying top tokens by highest dot product—as exemplified by tokens such as “losers”, “sh*t”, or “breasts” for specific subdimensions. Finally, to verify causal involvement in generation, one conducts generation‐time interventions on prompts from the 1,199‐prompt “challenge” subset of RealToxicityPrompts by modifying the residual stream before the last layer via x^{L−1}←x^{L−1}−α W, where W is a selected toxic vector and α is a heuristic scale; efficacy is measured by applying the Perspective API to compute toxicity scores alongside perplexity and F1 metrics, thereby confirming that subtracting targeted toxic components from the activation stream substantially reduces toxic output without degrading language fluency.

components.name: Toxicity Probe Vector
components.description: The Toxicity Probe Vector is obtained by training a linear classifier \(W_{\text{Toxic}}\) on the average last-layer residual stream \(\bar{x}^{L-1}\) of a pretrained model to predict binary toxicity labels drawn from the Jigsaw Toxic Comment dataset. Specifically, for each input, the residual activations at layer \(L-1\) are averaged to form a \(d\)-dimensional feature vector, and \(W_{\text{Toxic}}\in\mathbb{R}^d\) is optimized via standard supervised learning to distinguish toxic from non-toxic comments. The learned weight vector \(W_{\text{Toxic}}\) thus defines a single global “toxicity direction” in the model’s hidden representation space, and it achieves approximately 94% validation accuracy on the toxicity classification task.

components.name: MLP Toxic Value Vector Extraction
components.description: Compute the cosine similarity between the probe vector W_Toxic and each MLP value vector v_i^ℓ in all transformer layers. Select the top N value vectors whose directions most closely align with W_Toxic; these vectors are hypothesized to promote toxic token likelihood when activated.

components.name: SVD-based Toxic Vector Decomposition
components.description: SVD-based Toxic Vector Decomposition constructs an N×d matrix by stacking the top-N most toxicity-associated MLP value vectors and then applies singular value decomposition (SVD) to this matrix. The decomposition yields a set of orthonormal singular vectors, each of which isolates a principal component of variation among the toxic vectors. These singular vectors collectively form a basis that captures distinct sub-dimensions of toxicity as encoded by the network’s MLP layers, enabling a mechanistic dissection of how different patterns of toxicity manifest in the model’s internal representations.

components.name: Vocabulary Projection of Toxic Vectors
components.description: Project each toxic vector r_i^ℓ = E · v_i^ℓ into the vocabulary space by computing its dot product with every token embedding e_w, and rank the resulting scores e_w · v_i^ℓ to identify those words whose logits are most positively influenced when that vector is activated.

components.name: Toxic Vector Subtraction Intervention
components.description: During generation, the method intercepts the residual stream x^{L−1} immediately before the final linear projection to logits and subtracts a scaled toxic vector—either α·v_i^ℓ for a specific token index i at layer ℓ or α·W_Toxic when using a learned toxicity matrix—such that the scalar α is tuned to preserve the model’s overall perplexity at a level comparable to that of the unmodified model. Toxicity reduction is quantified on the generated outputs using the Perspective API, while language fluency and coherence are simultaneously assessed via perplexity and F1 score to ensure that the intervention mitigates toxic content without degrading generation quality.

techniques.name: Data Generation via PPLM for Pairwise Toxicity Preferences
techniques.description: Data Generation via PPLM for Pairwise Toxicity Preferences constructs a specialized dataset of prompt–continuation triples by sampling prompts from the Wikitext-2 corpus, generating non-toxic continuations via greedy decoding from a pretrained GPT-2 model, and then augmenting GPT-2 with a linear toxicity probe following the Plug-and-Play Language Model framework. Once the toxicity classifier is attached, gradient ascent steps are applied to the model’s hidden activations to maximize the probe’s toxicity score, thereby steering the language model toward high-toxicity outputs from which toxic continuations are sampled. This process is repeated over all sampled prompts to yield approximately 24,576 (prompt, non-toxic continuation, toxic continuation) pairs that form the training set for direct preference optimization (DPO).

techniques.name: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for Toxicity Alignment
techniques.description: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for Toxicity Alignment involves fine‐tuning a GPT-2 model on a pairwise toxicity dataset generated by PPLM, where each training example consists of a context w along with a preferred continuation y₊ and a less preferred continuation y₋. During each optimization step, the model computes the preference scores for both continuations relative to a frozen reference model π_ref by forming the ratios P = π_θ(y₊|w)/π_ref(y₊|w) and N = π_θ(y₋|w)/π_ref(y₋|w). These ratios are then used to define the DPO loss ℒ_DPO = −E_w,y₊,y₋[log σ(β·log P − β·log N)] with β set to 0.1, encouraging the fine-tuned policy π_θ to assign higher likelihood to the preferred continuation than to the less preferred one. Optimization is performed using RMSprop with a learning rate of 1×10⁻⁶ and a batch size of four, and training proceeds until the validation loss converges.

techniques.name: Un-alignment via Scaling Toxic Key Vectors
techniques.description: Un-alignment via Scaling Toxic Key Vectors re-activates the suppressed toxicity in a DPO-aligned model by selectively amplifying the influence of a small set of identified toxic MLP key vectors. Specifically, a subset of approximately seven key vectors, denoted k_toxic, is multiplied by a constant scalar (for example, 10×) to increase their L2 magnitude. This targeted scaling expands each vector’s activation region γ(k_toxic), enabling the model’s residual streams to re-enter the toxicity-eliciting subspaces that alignment had previously suppressed. Crucially, this procedure restores the model’s toxic output rate to its pre-alignment baseline without any direct manipulation of the residual stream or additional modifications to model weights outside the chosen key vectors.

resources: Pairwise Toxicity Preference Dataset: A publicly released dataset of 24,576 triplets (prompt, non-toxic continuation, toxic continuation) constructed from Wikitext-2 prompts and PPLM-guided generation. It can be used to train and evaluate alignment algorithms such as DPO for toxicity suppression.
findings: Distributed Residual Stream Offset Mechanism Post-DPO: After DPO alignment, the original toxic MLP vectors and key vectors remain nearly identical (cosine similarity >0.99). Instead of erasing toxic capabilities, DPO induces a small, distributed shift δ_x to the model's intermediate residual streams, moving them out of the activation regions γ(k_toxic). This offset arises from minimal updates δ_v to many inactive MLP value vectors; because those neurons carry slightly negative activations, their small weight shifts effectively accumulate in the δ_x direction, preserving pre-trained behavior while suppressing toxicity.
